coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescent molecularly imprinted photonic crystal hydrogel strip: Portable and efficient strategy for selective detection of tetracycline in foods of animal origin
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Mingfei
Wang, Yueyao
Yang, Jingying
Li, Huilin
Han, Xintong
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: Rapid, portable, and sensitive detection of tetracycline (TC) is crucial for the environment and human health. In this study, we developed carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescent molecularly imprinted photonic crystal hydrogel (FMIPH) strips for TC detection in animal-derived foods. CDs emit fluorescent signals, and molecularly imprinted polymers provide specific recognition sites for TC. Inverse opal photonic crystals afford stable 3D macroporous mass transfer channels that considerably reduce binding time between TC and the strips. The portable FMIPH strip exhibited a linear fluorescence response to TC in the concentration range of 0.1–50 μg mL−1, with a detection limit of 0.067 μg mL−1. Good recoveries of TC (93.86–112.59%) were observed in TC-spiked commercially available pork, eggs, and milk. A combination of FMIPH strips with a portable fluorescent reading device could achieve sensitive, on-site, and real-time detection of TC in animal-derived foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photonic crystals
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Tetracycline
Animal-derived foods

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of SiO2 and PC films
Preparation of FMIPH and fluorescent non-molecularly imprinted photonic crystal hydrogel (FNIPH) strips
TC detection using the CDs-based FMIPH strips
Principle of the CDs-based FMIPH strips for TC detection
Fluorescence detection of TC with FMIPH strips
Performance evaluation of the FMIPH strips
Analysis of TC in actual samples
Ethics approval and consent to participate

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Project Program of Key Laboratory of Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Tianjin Municipal Science and Technology Program

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272416 and 31972147 ), Project of Tianjin Science and Technology Plan (No. 22ZYJDSS00030 ), Project Program of Key Laboratory of Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, China (No. TJS202205 ) and Science and Technology Project of Hebei Education Department, China (No. QN2023124 ). The authors are grateful to Research Centre of Modern Analytical Technology (TUST) for their help in instrument support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation process of CDs-based FMIPH strips for TC detection.

(A) TEM image of CDs, inset: the particle size distribution. (B) FT-IR spectra of CDs. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths (320–400 nm). (D) Fluorescence excitation-emission matrix of CDs. (E) Fluorescence excitation spectra, fluorescence emission spectra, and UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, inset: the digital images of CDs under natural light and 365 nm UV light. (F) Calibration curves of fluorescence integrated area and UV absorbance of CD and reference material QS.
The SEM images of SiO2 (A), PC (B), FMIPH (C), FNIPH (D). The FT-IR (E) and XPS (F) spectra of FMIPH and FNIPH. (G) The C1s energy spectrum of FMIPH strips.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs-based FMIPH strips at different TC concentrations. (B) Standard curve of the fluorescence response of the CDs-based FMIPH strips and TC concentration (0.1–50 μg mL−1). (C) Adsorption kinetic curves of CDs-based FMIPH and FNIPH at different times. (D) Repeatability and (E) reproducibility of TC detection using CDs-based FMIPH strips. (F) Fluorescence response of CDs-based FMIPH strips to TC and different analytes (OTC, ENRO, CAP, and CIPRO) with the same concentration at 30 μg mL−1. (G) Fluorescence response of CDs-based FMIPH strips to mixed systems of TC and other competitors.

(A) UV–vis absorption and (B) fluorescence lifetimes of CDs before and after the addition of TC. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of TC and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of TC and CDs.
Detection of TC in commercially available animal-derived foods using the CDs-based FMIPH strip and HPLC method.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescent molecularly imprinted photonic crystal hydrogel strip: Portable and efficient strategy for selective detection of tetracycline in foods of animal origin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health of Tianjin, Tianjin University of Science & Technology, 300457 Tianjin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health of Tianjin

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health of Tianjin, Tianjin University of Science & Technology, 300457 Tianjin, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid, portable, and sensitive detection of tetracycline (TC) is crucial for the environment and human health. In this study, we developed carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescent molecularly imprinted photonic crystal hydrogel (FMIPH) strips for TC detection in animal-derived foods. CDs emit fluorescent signals, and molecularly imprinted polymers provide specific recognition sites for TC. Inverse opal photonic crystals afford stable 3D macroporous mass transfer channels that considerably reduce binding time between TC and the strips. The portable FMIPH strip exhibited a linear fluorescence response to TC in the concentration range of 0.1–50 μg mL−1, with a detection limit of 0.067 μg mL−1. Good recoveries of TC (93.86–112.59%) were observed in TC-spiked commercially available pork, eggs, and milk. A combination of FMIPH strips with a portable fluorescent reading device could achieve sensitive, on-site, and real-time detection of TC in animal-derived foods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics inhibit the normal growth and development of bacteria and other microorganisms. Some examples of antibiotics are tetracyclines (TC), sulfonamides, quinolones, and macrolides (Xu et al., 2023). TC is widely used in animal husbandry and aquaculture because of its good antibacterial activity, low price, and easy oral administration (Zhang et al., 2022a). However, TCs do not degrade easily in animals, and a large amount of the undegraded antibiotic is excreted into the environment and enters the human body through biological enrichment (Peng et al., 2022). A small amount of the ingested TC accumulates in animals and passes through into animal-derived foods, which adversely affect human teeth and bones and damage the liver and kidneys (Warner et al., 2022). In addition, TC drugs destroy the normal flora in the human body and lead to the production of drug-resistant bacteria, thus weakening the immune system (Li et al., 2020; Osorio et al., 2023). Hence, monitoring and detecting the trace TC content in animal-derived foods is crucial. Microbiological inhibition (Astudillo et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2020), electrophoresis (Deng et al., 2012), HPLC (Zergiebel et al., 2023), and LC-MS (Varenina et al., 2023) are common strategies for detecting TC. Even though these methods are highly sensitive, they require expensive equipment, professional operators, complicated pretreatment, and considerable time for their operation, which limits their practical applications for real-time, low-cost, and in situ detection. Thus, a simple, rapid, portable, sensitive, and efficient method is necessary for the quantitation of TC residues in foods.

Fluorescence sensing is a well-known qualitative and quantitative analytical method that depends on the change in fluorescence intensity or color caused by a specific reaction between the target and a fluorescent source. It is a promising analytical method owing to its simple operation, fast response time, and high sensitivity. Various fluorescent substances such as quantum dots (He et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022b), metal nanoparticles (Dadmehr et al., 2023), and organic dyes (Guan et al., 2021) have been used to construct fluorescent probes with different properties. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have attracted attention owing to their excellent fluorescence properties, low cost, and simple preparation (Huang et al., 2019) and have been applied in biological imaging (Xu et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2023), photocatalysis (Zhang et al., 2022c), and nano-sensing (Keerthana et al., 2023). Although CDs-based fluorescent probes have a highly sensitive fluorescence response to targets, they have significant issues. As fluorescent probes are not specific, their anti-interference ability is insufficient to analyze complex food samples.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are synthetic polymers with specific recognition sites that are complementary to target molecules, similar to antibodies and aptamers. They can be prepared in large quantities with low cost and good stability through molecular imprinting technology and have wide applications in chromatographic separation, bionic recognition, and nano-sensing (Chen et al., 2021; Hong et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022). Fluorescent MIPs can convert the binding between the imprinted sites and the target into fluorescent signals, and the changes in the fluorescence signal are used for quantitation of the target. To date, several CDs@MIPs fluorescence sensors have been reported (Hu et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2022).

Photonic crystals (PCs) are dielectric materials with a highly ordered spatial structure and a wide bandgap range. PCs with different bandgaps exhibit different structural colors according to the Bragg diffraction effect (Fathi et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022; Qiu et al., 2022). In addition, three-dimensional inverse opal PCs (IOPCs) have a 3D macroporous structure, which provides favorable mass transfer channels for the diffusion and adsorption of the target, thus improving the detection speed and sensitivity (Zhu et al., 2019). PCs-based sensors have been successfully applied in the rapid screening and analysis of pesticide molecules (Wang et al.,2019), metal ions (Taha et al., 2022), spices (Zhang et al., 2019), and other analytes. Combining PCs with fluorescent MIPs not only shortens the response time but also increases the number and spatial arrangement order of specific binding sites to further improve the specific recognition ability of the sensors.

In this study, a portable fluorescent molecularly imprinted photonic crystal hydrogel (FMIPH) strip coated with CDs was developed for the rapid, accurate, and sensitive detection of TC residues in animal-derived foods. In particular, the CDs were used to provide stable fluorescence for the FMIPH. The MIPs provided specific recognition sites for TC recognition as bionic recognition elements. The 3D macroporous structure of PCs not only provided favorable mass transfer channels for target adsorption and diffusion but also increased the density and uniform distribution of specific recognition sites in the molecularly imprinted system. The CDs-based FMIPH strips developed in this study displayed good fluorescence response and selectivity towards TC, providing a portable and effective strategy for the rapid screening and detection of TC residues in animal-derived foods.

The standards TC, oxytetracycline (OTC), enrofloxacin (ENRO), chloramphenicol (CAP), and ciprofloxacin (CIPRO) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, o-phenylenediamine, ammonia, quinine sulfate (QS), methacrylic acid (MAA), and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) used for CDs and MIP synthesis were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The crosslinker ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) and polymerization initiator azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) were purchased from TCI Shanghai Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Solvent-concentrated H2SO4, H2O2, and hydrofluoric acid were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent No.1 Factory (Tianjin, China). Sodium chloride, glacial acetic acid, methanol, ethanol, and acetic acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was obtained from a Milli-Q Purification System (18 MΩ·cm, Millipore, USA). Ordinary glass pieces and Plexiglas PMMA (38.0 × 12.5 × 1.0 mm; Shenzhen, China) were used to prepare the FMIPH strips.

A muffle furnace (BF51894C-1, Thermo, USA) was used for the synthesis of CDs. A blast dryer (DHG-2000, HBYQ, China) was used for the self-assembly of PCs. A water bath (SY-1–2, Honour, China) was used for the thermal polymerization of FMIPH. The characteristic absorption peaks of CDs and TC were recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Evolution 300, Thermo, USA). The fluorescence intensity of CDs and FMIPH were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (LUMINA, Thermo, USA). The size and structure of the materials were observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Apreo, Thermo, USA) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Talos G2 200X, Thermo, USA). The surface functional groups of CDs were analyzed using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, IS50, Nicolet, USA). The elemental composition and molecular structure of materials were characterized by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo, USA). An HPLC system (LC-20AT, Agilent, USA) was used to verify the TC content in spiked foods.

The fluorescent CDs were synthesized via a modified hydrothermal method reported in the literature (Xie et al., 2022; Han et al., 2019). Briefly, 0.46 g of citric acid and 0.21 g of o-phenylenediamine were accurately weighed and added to a glass flask containing 10.0 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was ultrasonicated to obtain a homogeneous solution, transferred to a muffle furnace lined with Teflon, and heated to 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting yellow liquid was removed and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialyzed (1000 Da) for 24 h. The obtained yellow CDs stock solution was stored at 4 °C in the dark for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of SiO2 and PC films
ce:section.ce:para: The SiO2 nanoparticles were synthesized via a modified Stöber method. Briefly, 40.0 mL of ethanol, 7.7 mL of ammonia, and 2.23 mL of ultrapure water were added to a chromic acid-treated round-bottom flask and stirred well. Ethanol (50.0 mL) and 2.29 mL of TEOS were added to a 100.0 mL centrifuge tube, stirred, and quickly added to the above-mentioned round bottom flask. The mixed solution was stirred magnetically at room temperature for 12 h, centrifuged three times at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and washed with anhydrous ethanol. After removing the excess solution, the white precipitate was dried in a blast dryer at 50 °C for 12 h and ground into a fine powder.

Ordinary glass pieces were soaked in Piranha solution for 24 h, successively ultrasonicated with deionized water and ethanol, dried in an N2 flow, and placed aside for use. SiO2 powder (50.0 mg) was dispersed in 6.34 mL of ethanol to prepare a suspension of 1% SiO2 nanoparticles. Then, the hydrophilic glass sheet was inserted vertically into the SiO2 suspension to allow the SiO2 to sink vertically and self-assemble. The ethanol was completely evaporated using a blast dryer at 50 °C to afford the final PC films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of FMIPH and fluorescent non-molecularly imprinted photonic crystal hydrogel (FNIPH) strips
ce:section.ce:para: Accurately weighed template TC (44.44 mg; 0.1 mmol) was dissolved in 5.0 mL of methanol–water solution (9:1, v/v). The CDs dispersion (1.0 mL) and 67.78 μL (0.8 mmol) of the functional monomer MAA were added successively into the solution. The mixture was homogenized via ultrasonication and placed in the dark overnight at 4 °C. Finally, 1.13 mL (6 mmol) of the cross-linker EGDMA and 12.0 mg of the initiator AIBN were added and dispersed homogeneously by ultrasonication. N2 was passed through this solution for 10 min, and the obtained fluorescent molecular imprinting pre-polymerization solution was stored at 4 °C in the dark. The fluorescent non-molecular imprinting pre-polymerization solution was prepared via the same procedure, except for the template addition step.

The PMMA sheet was laminated on the surface of the glass sheet with the PC film, and the two ends were anchored to form a sandwich structure. The fluorescent molecular imprinting/non-molecular imprinting pre-polymerization solution (20.0 μL) was slowly injected into the sandwich gap, protected by N2, sealed, and thermally polymerized in a water bath at 60 °C for 4 h. After polymerization, the dovetail clips were removed, and the sandwich structures were immersed in 5% HF until the glass sheet peeled off. The PMMA sheet was soaked for a further 12 h to elute the SiO2 template. The PMMA sheet was repeatedly rinsed with ultrapure water to remove the residual HF. The template was eluted with methanol:acetic acid (9:1, v/v) for 3 h (the eluent was changed every 1 h) and repeatedly rinsed with methanol to wash off the residual acetic acid on the surface. After sufficient rinsing with ultrapure water to remove the residual methanol, the obtained FMIPH and FNIPH strips were stored in deionized water before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TC detection using the CDs-based FMIPH strips
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-based FMIPH strips were placed in a cuvette containing 5.0 mL of the TC-methanol solution of different concentrations (0.1, 1, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μg mL−1) and incubated for 15 min at room temperature in the dark. Fluorescence spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm and a scanning range of 440–540 nm. The voltage was set to 550 V, and the slit width was 10 nm.

Pork, milk, and eggs, which are susceptible to TC contamination, were selected for evaluating the feasibility and accuracy of the developed CDs-based FMIPH strips for TC detection. Samples purchased from a local supermarket (Tianjin, China) were crushed and homogenized. Each homogenized sample was accurately weighed (5.0 g) and spiked with TC at three concentrations (10, 30, and 50 μg g−1). The spiked samples were vortexed and left to rest overnight under natural conditions. Certain volumes (first: 20.0 mL; second: 20.0 mL; third: 10.0 mL) of Mcllvaine-Na2EDTA buffer solution were used to extract TC from the samples. The mixture was vortexed, sonicated, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatants obtained in the three extraction steps were combined, fixed to 50.0 mL, and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The obtained sample pretreatment solutions were measured TC amount using the newly developed CDs-based FMIPH strips.

The spiked pork, eggs, and milk were extracted and cleaned according to the National Standard of the People's Republic of China (GB/T 21317–2007). A UV detector was used with a detection wavelength of 350 nm. The C18 column (150 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 μm) was used with a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. The injection volume was 50 μL, and the column temperature was 30 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the CDs-based FMIPH strips for TC detection
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the schematic of the preparation procedure for CDs-based FMIPH strips. First, CDs that emit blue fluorescence under UV light were synthesized via a hydrothermal method, and SiO2 microspheres at 280 nm were synthesized via the Stöber method. PCs were obtained via self-assembly through inserting the hydrophilic glass sheet vertically in an ethanol suspension of 1% SiO2 microspheres. A molecularly imprinted pre-polymerization solution containing the template TC, the functional monomer MAA, the cross-linker EGDMA, and the initiator AIBN was thermally polymerized in the sandwich interlayer formed by the PCs and PMMA. Removal of SiO2 resulted in the formation of a 3D inverse opal framework structure. Finally, the template molecule TC was eluted to form cavities that are spatially complementary to the target molecule and that have multiple sites of action. These cavities on the FMIPH strips recognize and adsorb the target TC with high selectivity and specificity, causing fluorescence quenching. The change in fluorescence intensity of these CDs-based FMIPH strips enables the rapid and efficient quantitation of the TC target.

The performance of CDs, which are the fluorescence source of the hydrogel strips, is crucial for the sensitivity and accuracy of the detection process. Fig. 2 shows the morphology and spectroscopic characterization results of the CDs. The TEM image and particle size distribution of the CDs (Fig. 2 A) show that the synthesized CDs were uniformly dispersed and of the same size, with an average particle size of 1.729 nm. The broad FT-IR peaks (Fig. 2 B) at 3532 cm−1 corresponded to NH and OH stretching vibrations, whereas the peaks at 2924, 1631, and 1222 cm−1 corresponded to CH, CO, and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1384 and 747 cm−1 corresponded to the CH in-plane and out-plane bending vibrations, respectively. This suggests that the surface of the prepared CDs was rich in hydrophilic functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, which provided good aqueous affinity to the CDs.

The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were recorded at various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2 C), where the fluorescence intensity increased significantly from 320 to 370 nm but decreased from 370 to 400 nm. Therefore, 370 nm was considered as the optimal excitation wavelength. However, the emission wavelength of the CDs did not change at any excitation wavelength and was maintained at 465 nm. This indicates that the photoluminescence of the synthesized CDs is independent of the excitation wavelength (Wang et al., 2023; Ren and Fan, 2023). The excitation-emission matrix (Fig. 2 D) confirmed an optimal fluorescence intensity at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm and an emission wavelength of 465 nm. As shown in Fig. 2 E, the optimal excitation wavelength of the CDs was consistent with their UV absorption wavelength, and the CDs emitted bright blue light under UV light irradiation (inset of Fig. 2 E). The luminescence capacity of the fluorescent CDs was evaluated by analyzing the fluorescence quantum yield with QS as the reference. Good fluorescence emission was observed up to 43.87% of QS (Fig. 2 F). Additionally, the fluorescence stability and photobleaching resistance of the CDs were investigated. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs under light-protected conditions remained almost unchanged for 10 days with an RSD of 2.27% (n = 6) (Fig. S1). Similarly, after storing the CDs in natural light for 10 days, their fluorescence was maintained at 79% of the original intensity (Fig. S2). Considering the complexity of food substrates, the salt resistance of the CDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was stable at NaCl concentration as high as 10 mM with an RSD of only 2.58 % for six measurements.

Fig. 3
A and 3B show the SEM images of the synthesized SiO2, PC. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the prepared SiO2 particles were spheres of uniform size with an average particle size of 280 nm. The self-assembled SiO2 particles on the PCs were ordered, showing a hexagonal face-centered cubic structure, which provided a good framework for preparing inverse opal structures (Fig. 3 B). Fig. 3 C and 3D show the SEM images of FMIPH and FNIPH strips, respectively. It could be seen that after removing the SiO2 template, FMIPH exhibited a neat 3D macroporous structure, whereas FNIPH showed a complex and disordered skeleton structure due to the absence of the template molecules. The FT-IR spectra show that FMIPH and FNIPH had similar absorption peaks, indicating that the template TC in FMIPH had been mostly eluted. Among them, the peaks at 2989, and 2951 cm−1 corresponded to the CH stretching vibration, whereas the peaks at 1442, and 983 cm−1 corresponded to the in-plane bending vibration and the out-of-plane bending vibration of CH, respectively. And the peak at 1725 cm−1 corresponds to the CO stretching vibration and the peaks at 1240, 1142 cm−1 belong to the COC stretching vibration, indicating the presence of EGDMA and MAA, which in turn justified the successful preparation of FMIPH and FNIPH (Fig. 3 E). Fig. 3 F shows that the peaks at 284.8, 399.7, and 531.7 eV corresponded to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, revealing the presence of C, N, and O elements. FMIPH and FNIPH exhibited almost identical peak patterns, indicating that the template on FMIPH was successfully eluted. At the XPS energy spectrum for C1s, the peaks at 284.6, 285.1, and 285.9 eV corresponded to CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO, respectively, proving the successful preparation of FMIPH (Fig. 3 G).

In this study, CDs were applied as fluorescent sources for the construction of FMIPH strips. The volume of CDs was directly related to the fluorescence performance of the FMIPH strips, which further affects the sensitivity of TC detection. As the volume of the CDs increased, the imprinting factor (IF) initially increased and then decreased (Fig. S4 ). IF reached its maximum value (3.362) at the CDs volume of 1.00 mL. Moreover, the template/functional monomer/cross-linker molar ratio is crucial for obtaining a 3D macroporous structure with strong and specific adsorption properties. IF reached a maximum of 3.765 at a TC:MAA:EGDMA molar ratio of 1:8:60 (Fig. S5 and S6), indicating that the FMIPH strips prepared under these conditions formed a stable 3D skeletal structure and abundant recognition sites, which facilitated the specific recognition and binding of TC target molecules.

After molecular imprinting thermal polymerization, the effective elution of fixed template molecules is not only conducive to the formation of specific binding cavities and recognition sites but also avoids the leakage of the template molecules during rebinding, resulting in false-positive results. Hence, methanol/acetic acid (9:1, v/v) was used as eluent to compare the elution effect of different elution times on FMIPH strips. As shown in Fig. S7, after the third elution, the UV absorption peak of TC at 360 nm nearly disappeared, indicating that the TC template in the imprinting system was completely removed. Therefore, three elution times were considered optimal for the analytical method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of TC with FMIPH strips
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence response of the developed FMIPH strip to different TC concentrations was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4
A, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly with increasing TC concentration (0.1–50 μg mL−1). The fluorescence intensity changed significantly even at the low TC concentration of 0.1 μg mL−1. Fig. 4 B shows the relationship between the fluorescence response of FMIPH to different TC concentrations, conforming to the Stern–Volmer equation: F 0 /F = K SV C TC + 1, where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of FMIPH before and after the addition of TC, respectively; K SV is the quenching constant; and C TC is the TC concentration. Good linearity between the FMIPH fluorescence response and TC concentration was achieved in the wide range of 0.1–50 μg mL−1 with an R 2 of 0.9948. The limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) was calculated to be 0.067 μg mL−1. These results indicated that the developed FMIPH strips can analyze the target TC sensitively and accurately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance evaluation of the FMIPH strips
ce:section.ce:para: Adsorption kinetics experiments were performed by monitoring the fluorescence response of the CDs-based FMIPH strips immersed in a TC–methanol solution (30 μg mL−1) for different times (1–30 min). The fluorescence response of FMIPH strips to TC was significantly higher than that to the non-specific adsorption of TC on the FNIPH strips, owing to the large number of specific recognition sites in the former (Fig. 4 C). The fluorescent response of FMIPH gradually increased as the soaking time was increased to 15 min, when the F 0 /F value reached a maximum value and gradually stabilized, indicating an adsorption equilibrium. However, the fluorescent response of FNIPH reached stability after 25 min, further indicating that the 3D macroporous structure of FMIPH provided mass transfer channels for diffusion and adsorption of the target, shortening the reaction time.

Fig. S8 shows the fluorescence intensity of the prepared FMIPH strips within 25 days. The RSDs of the fluorescence intensity (F 0) obtained within 25 days were only 2.8%, indicating its good storage stability. The fluorescence intensity of FMIPH strips under different ionic strengths and pH remained stable with RSDs of 0.87% and 3.14%, respectively, indicating that they could cope with changes in ionic strength and pH of the external environment (Fig. S9 and S10). The repeatability of the fluorescence response of a single FMIPH strip towards TC was investigated. The fluorescence response (F 0 /F) of the strip decreased slightly after each elution, but retained 84.26% of the initial value after 5 cycles of adsorption-elution, indicating that FMIPH had good repeatability (Fig. 4D). The fluorescence responses of the FMIPH strips from different batches were compared to evaluate the reproducibility of the preparation procedure. The F 0 /F value of the FMIPH strips prepared from each batch towards a TC concentration of 30 μg mL−1 was averaged at 1.684 with an RSD of 1.20% (n = 5; Fig. 4 E).

To verify the selectivity of the FMIPH strips for TC, the fluorescence response of FMIPH to different analytes (OTC, ENRO, CAP, and CIPRO) was studied. The FMIPH strips showed the best fluorescence response to TC among the analytes (Fig. 4 F). This was attributed to the synergistic interaction between the specific recognition sites of molecularly imprinted polymers and the high mass transfer channels of the IOPCs. Mixture systems were formed by mixing TC with other competitors, and the anti-interference ability of FMIPH strips was assessed by determining the fluorescence response of FMIPH. The results showed that the fluorescence response obtained by FMIPH in the mixed system was not significantly different from that in a single TC solution, but was significantly higher than that of the single competitor (Fig. 4 G). This indicated that the recognition of TC by the FMIPH reagent strip was not interfered by other competitors, confirming the anti-interference ability of the FMIPH reagent strip. This result is favorable for the application of FMIPH strips for TC detection in complex food matrices.

Fluorescence quenching mechanisms mainly include static quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and internal filtration effect (IFE) (Wen et al., 2023). As CDs are the fluorescence source of the FMIPH strips, we investigated their optical properties to elucidate the quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. 5
A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs was almost unchanged after the addition of TC, indicating that no new substance was produced and eliminating the possibility of static quenching. Fig. 5 B shows that the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs decreased from 3.78 to 3.20 ns after TC addition, suggesting that the energy of fluorescence emission was transferred to the quencher and that there might exist FRET or PET interactions, eliminating the possibility of IFE as the quenching mechanism. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of TC and the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs only partially overlapped (Fig. 5 C), eliminating the possibility of FRET as the quenching mechanism (Wu et al., 2023). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of CDs by TC was presumed to be caused by PET. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and TC were analyzed, and it was found that CDs and TC had similar absorption band gaps. The charge could be transferred from CDs to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of TC, thereby quenching the fluorescence of CDs, further verifying the possibility of PET (Fig. 5 D) (Zhou et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of TC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the practical applicability of the developed FMIPH strips, the commercially available animal-derived foods pork, eggs, and milk were spiked with three TC concentrations of 10, 30, and 50 μg g−1 for the fluorescence analysis of the developed FMIPH strips, which were validated by HPLC. The recoveries of TC were calculated according to the following equation, where C d and C s meant the detected and spiked concentration of TC, respectively.
Recovery
( % )
=
C d
C s
× 100 %

The recoveries of all samples with different TC concentrations ranged from 93.86 to 112.59% with an RSD less than 4.8% (n = 3; Table 1 ). These results were highly correlated with those of conventional HPLC, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9812, indicating the high reliability of the developed FMIPH strips for TC detection in actual samples (Fig. S11). The developed FMIPH strips are portable and can finish the entire fluorescence analysis process within 15 min, which is convenient for on-site, real-time, and rapid detection. Table S1 shows the merits of the other reported detection strategies for TC, demonstrating that the CDs-based FMIPH strips can be used for rapid, convenient, accurate, and sensitive detection of target TC residues in foods.

In this study, portable CDs-based FMIPH strips were developed for the detection of TC residues in animal-derived foods. The CDs in the FMIPH strips exhibit highly sensitive fluorescence, the MIPs show a specific recognition ability for TC, and the IOPCs provide mass transfer channels and orderly arranged sites. All these aspects of the FMIPH strips enable high detection sensitivity, high specificity, and a short operation time, making them an efficient TC detection tool for rapid and low-cost screening of TC residues in foods. Such portable, fluorescent, biomimetic detection strips can also be developed for other targets in food and environmental samples and combined with portable fluorescent reading devices to achieve on-site and real-time detection of the targets.

This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272416 and 31972147), Project of Tianjin Science and Technology Plan (No. 22ZYJDSS00030), Project Program of Key Laboratory of Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, China (No. TJS202205) and Science and Technology Project of Hebei Education Department, China (No. QN2023124). The authors are grateful to Research Centre of Modern Analytical Technology (TUST) for their help in instrument support.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.

Mingfei Pan: Resources, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Yueyao Wang: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation. Jingying Yang: Data curation. Huilin Li: Formal analysis. Xintong Han: Software, Data curation. Shuo Wang: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137407.
